--- SOURCE 1: Biomechanics in Sport: Pivotal in Optimizing Performance ---
URL: https://www.amu.apus.edu/area-of-study/health-sciences/resources/biomechanics-in-sport/

SUMMARY:
Applying biomechanics in sport can help coaches achieve optimum performance from their athletes. From basic biomechanical principles to Olympic-level technique, skill acquisition and development, coaches can guide players in developing the correct movements for a sport and avoid sports injuries. For instance, a coach watches the athlete to gauge the athlete’s current skills and note possible errors involving sports biomechanics and the athlete’s technique. By utilizing correct biomechanical analysis, sports science professionals such as coaches or personal trainers can more easily see in what areas athletes need to improve their performance. Coaches should divide the athletic movements specific to a sport into separate components that can be performed safely, so that an athlete's flaws may be addressed with simple coaching cues relative to optimum skill acquisition.

FULL CONTENT:
![AMU - American Military University - A member of the accredited American Public University System](/images/site/logos/amu-logo-full-white.svg)

# Biomechanics in Sport: Pivotal in Optimizing Performance

By [Dr. Barry Shollenberger](#about-authors)  |  10/10/2024

![coach point at net and basketball player preparing to shoot](/images/site/amu/biomechanics-in-sports.jpg)

Applying biomechanics in sport can help coaches achieve optimum performance from their athletes. From basic biomechanical principles to Olympic-level technique, skill acquisition and development, coaches can guide players in developing the correct movements for a sport and avoid sports injuries.

## Understanding Biomechanics in Sports

Biomechanical principles in sports are nothing more than the basic rules of mechanics and physics that lead to optimum movement of an athlete’s body.

Besides gravity and air resistance, there are many forces on earth that affect human movement, especially when an athlete using sport and exercise equipment. As a result, a knowledge of what these forces do in relation to athletic performance can be instrumental in raising the standard of performance in every sport.

Biomechanics in sport involves various concepts such as:

World-class athletes in all sports use superior technique based on biomechanical principles that control human movement. Their skills are developed and practiced over time until they can be performed without a second thought. Coaching is involved in all stages of these movements, from acquisition and development of skills to improving sports performance.

Many amateur athletes can benefit from observing accomplished athletes in their sport and imitating their body movement patterns. But for coaches, this type of skill acquisition should be combined with critical observation.

## Errors in Sports Biomechanics Lead to the Development of Inferior Skills and Potential Injuries

Inefficient athletic movements not only lead to inferior sports skills but also could lead to serious injuries. [Correct biomechanics](https://us.humankinetics.com/blogs/excerpt/apply-biomechanics-to-improve-techniques) are the key to optimum athletic positions and, by extension, to improved performance.

Biomechanical principles can be seen as the effects of energy and forces on the motion of physical objects. For coaching purposes, this definition includes human movement and optimum sport biomechanics. Good technique in sport is based on making the best use of sound biomechanical analytics and principles.

Balance and stability can be the key to optimum sport performance. The back-and-forth flow of maximum stability to a temporary minimum stability often defines how movements are performed by an athlete. An athlete constantly applies the forces of appropriate muscles to regain balance and stability.

For example, an Olympic gymnast on a balance beam may perform a routine involving turns, rolls, and handsprings using several upper body muscles. That athlete then uses lower-body muscles to land in a stable manner on a padded surface after a dismount.

## Evaluating Athletic Performance with the Aid of Biomechanics

Coaches evaluate and improve an athlete’s skills through observation. For instance, a coach watches the athlete to gauge the athlete’s current skills and note possible errors involving sports biomechanics and the athlete’s technique.

A good plan is essential for this sports biomechanics evaluation. First, the coach should view the athlete’s entire skill set several times before zeroing in on suspected biomechanical errors. Next, the coach should try to observe a motion from several angles to determine the best vantage point.

Finally, any electronic aids should be fixed at the points that are best for recording the athlete’s movements. Slow-motion video can be a significant asset, because it allows movement to be recorded and replayed for a more effective evaluation. The coach can then communicate with the athlete to relay the areas of concern or faulty biomechanics.

Sports biomechanics that need to be corrected should be divided into four primary stages:

Each phase should be addressed sequentially, highlighting the most egregious errors first. Dealing with the most obvious flaw first will prevent athletes from suffering “analysis paralysis” that occurs when multiple biomechanical principles are corrected simultaneously.

## Newton’s Laws of Motion: How They Impact Sports Biomechanics and Athletic Performance

For sound biomechanical techniques in human movement, an understanding of the basic Newtonian concepts of inertia, acceleration, and action/reaction is essential.

All athletes and objects have mass and inertia. Athletes use their muscles as the physical force to overcome inertia and perform skills. Just a few examples include throwing a football, hitting a pitch with a baseball bat, or jumping over a hurdle.

An athlete’s acceleration of his or her body parts is proportional to the force applied to movement. This law can be expressed as force = mass x acceleration.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Human athletic movements can involve acceleration, deceleration, or zero acceleration (either the athlete is motionless or moving at a uniform rate).

The forces that cause action and reaction are also essential to an understanding of preferred body movement technique. The physical force exerted by athletes can be expected to be met with a reaction that is equal in force and in the opposite direction. A good example of this physical force would be when a high jumper pushes down (plants) his lead foot, the ground pushes back with an equal but opposite force to propel the jumper upward.

Overcoming the universal forces of motion, gravity, friction, and air resistance are essential in completing most movements in sports. By utilizing correct biomechanical analysis, sports science professionals such as coaches or personal trainers can more easily see in what areas athletes need to improve their performance.

## Continuing Professional Development Is Also Helpful in Sports Biomechanics and Enhanced Sports Performance

How does a coach determine the correct techniques for their athletes to optimize error-free performance? An often-questionable approach is to have players try to mimic world-class athletes with the hope that they will also perform at an elite athlete level. It is much better to break down the components of optimum performance enhancement and use them as teachable moments for athletes.

Determining the correct movement techniques needed to complete athletic skills is the crux of delivering error-free performance improvement by an athlete. To become skilled in the process, coaches must keep up with the latest research in their sport.

In addition, coaches should attend clinics, seminars, and workshops to remain up to date with the latest coaching technology and sports performance. Finally, coaches should access current technology in their sport to stay on the cutting edge of computer and video advances that relate to the biomechanics of human movement.

## Sport-Specific Coaching Points for Skill Acquisition

There are many biomechanical terms that are related to coaching points to be shared with developing athletes. Some of these terms include:

How these terms are utilized, however, is different for each sport. For instance, a baseball player and a football player can throw a ball to another player, but the sports equipment and the movements used by each player are different due to the sport.

Locker room walls are excellent places for charts, diagrams, images, and other instructional material demonstrating the correct performance of movement techniques related to a specific sport. Athletes can be constantly reminded of how they should perform certain skills correctly, and they will also find it easy to check on any new techniques to be perfected.

Full-length mirrors are also helpful when it comes to analyzing human motion. Mirrors can help aspiring athletes to easily watch their own movements, achieve better physical fitness, receive immediate feedback to improve performance and reduce injury risk.

Coaches should divide the athletic movements specific to a sport into separate components that can be performed safely, so that an athlete's flaws may be addressed with simple coaching cues relative to optimum skill acquisition. These components should be the most important ones in a sport and are the ones most often violated by players.

Sports science cues (coaching points) can be verbal and used in practice sessions or games. Care should be taken not to overload an athlete with too many cues to improve performance, especially in a competitive situation. This method is the safest way to ensure optimal body movements by athletes and ensure injury prevention.

## Correcting Biomechanical Errors to Avoid Athletic Injuries

It is virtually impossible to devise a single method that works for the correction of all biomechanical errors committed by athletes in a particular sport. Coaches should always be mindful that an athlete’s initial attempts at skill improvement is best attempted at a low speed and minimum effort. This strategy will ensure that any injury caused by different human movements will not occur.

Some sports biomechanics lend themselves better than others for preventing [accidental sports injuries](http://baseballreflections.com/2017/03/22/proper-biomechanics-can-prevent-sports-injuries-pitching-edition/) sustained while attempting movement technique improvements. Spotting in gymnastics, for example, helps to protect athletes. At the same time, knowing that there is a spotter gives them confidence to explore the acquisition of new skills and improving their performance.

There are any rarely any athletes who avoid injuries. If these injuries are determined by a medical orthopedic to be caused by faulty biomechanics, then it is the responsibility of the coaching staff to make sure there is no recurrence of the injuries during and after rehabilitation efforts.

When previously injured athletes return to training, they should resume training by using fundamental human motion patterns before moving to more complex skills. The causes of the initial injury should always be considered to avoid any activities that might aggravate the athlete's well-being.

The steps of rehabilitation correction should begin with separating the faulty phase of the movement from the overall skill. The faulty phase should be broken down into its biomechanical components and specific exercises or activities designed to strengthen the athlete’s performance.

Next, the skill should be performed and mastered slowly before the athlete attempts the complete skill at reduced speed. Finally, the athlete can then increase speed, duration, and effort, providing that the injury does not recur.

## Sports and Health Sciences Degrees at AMU

For sports science professionals seeking to gain the knowledge to pursue coaching or personal training opportunities, American Military University (AMU) offers two degrees:

Courses in these programs are taught by experienced health sciences professionals. These courses include topics such as:

For more information about these degree programs, [visit AMU’s sports and health sciences program page](/area-of-study/health-sciences/).

Dr. Barry Shollenberger is an associate professor in the sports management program at American Military University. He holds a B.A. in General Studies from Moravian College, an M.A. in Education from Western Kentucky University, and an Ed.D. in Health, Physical Education and Recreation from The University of Alabama. He has coached baseball for 30 years, 15 of which were at The University of Alabama where he was recognized as the College Baseball Coach of the Year by Baseball America in 1983.

[American Military University](https://www.amu.apus.edu/)

--- SOURCE 2: How Sports Biomechanics Can Help Athletes ---
URL: https://theathletenow.com/how-sports-biomechanics-can-help-athletes/

SUMMARY:
Enhanced Performance: By refining your technique, you can achieve more efficient and effective movements, leading to improved performance in

FULL CONTENT:
![Athlete Now Orange Primary Logo](https://theathletenow.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/cropped-AN-Primary-Orange-1.png)

# How Sports Biomechanics Can Help Athletes

![Female athlete with motion capture sensors on her body running in biomechanical lab. Recording the movement and performance of sportswoman in sports science lab.](https://theathletenow.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/iStock-904892112.jpg "How Sports Biomechanics Can Help Athletes 1")

## Introduction to Sports Biomechanics

Sports biomechanics is a scientific discipline that applies principles of mechanics to understand and improve human movement in sports. By analyzing the forces and motions involved in athletic activities, sports biomechanists help athletes optimize their performance and reduce the risk of injury. This field bridges the gap between sports science and practical application, providing invaluable insights for athletes, coaches, and practitioners.

## Table of Contents

## What to Expect When Working with a Sports Biomechanist

## Benefits of Working with a Sports Biomechanist

### Integrating Motor Learning and Skill Refinement

Biomechanists also play a crucial role in motor learning and skill refinement. By understanding the neuromuscular coordination involved in specific movements, they can help athletes develop more efficient motor patterns. This involves breaking down complex skills into simpler components, providing targeted feedback, and using drills to reinforce correct movement patterns. The result is improved skill acquisition and more effective execution of sports techniques.

## Understanding Kinetics and Kinematics

### How Do Biomechanists Apply The Kinetics and Kinematics in Sport

![How sports biomechanics can help](http://theathletenow.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/iStock-1246037535.jpg "How Sports Biomechanics Can Help Athletes 2")

In a sporting environment, biomechanists use tools like motion capture, force plates, and high-speed cameras to analyse an athlete’s performance. This detailed analysis helps identify inefficiencies and areas for improvement. For instance, by examining joint angles and movement velocities, they can suggest changes to enhance movement efficiency and reduce energy expenditure, leading to better performance and reduced fatigue.

### Equipment, Footwear and Ergonomics

Sports biomechanists also study the effects of equipment, footwear, and ergonomics on performance and health. By analysing how different gear influences movement and force distribution, they can recommend the most suitable equipment for each athlete. For instance, selecting the right shoes can enhance running efficiency and reduce injury risk, while optimising ergonomic equipment, like custom-fitted bikes or tennis rackets, can improve performance and comfort. This expertise ensures athletes make informed choices about their gear, maximising both safety and performance.

## How Sports Biomechanists Help Athletes Compared to Other Practitioners

### How Do Biomechanists Differ from Sports Physiologists

While sports physiology focuses on the biological processes within the body during exercise (such as cardiovascular function, muscle metabolism, and energy production), sports biomechanics centers on the mechanics of movement. Sports physiologists study how the body responds to physical activity on a cellular and systemic level, whereas sports biomechanists examine how forces interact with the body to produce movement. Both fields are crucial, but biomechanists provides a detailed analysis of movement patterns, which is essential for technique optimisation and injury prevention.

### How Sports Biomechanics Relates to Strength and Conditioning

Strength and conditioning coaches focus on developing physical attributes like strength, power, and endurance through training programs. Sports biomechanics, on the other hand, analyzes how these attributes are applied in motion. While strength and conditioning improve the capacity of muscles and systems, biomechanics refines how these capacities are used in specific sports movements. The two disciplines overlap in their aim to enhance performance but differ in their methods and areas of focus.

## What Qualifications Do Sports Biomechanists Have?

Sports biomechanists typically have advanced degrees (BSc hons or MSC) in biomechanics, sports science, kinesiology, or related fields. Their training includes learning and assessments that integrates physics, anatomy, physiology, sports injuries, sports performance monitoring and engineering principles. Many also have experience in sports science research and practical application, often working closely with athletes, coaches, and other practitioners.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) How Sports Biomechanics Can Help Athletes

### **How does sports biomechanics differ from physical therapy?**

While physical therapy focuses on rehabilitation and recovery from injury, sports biomechanics aims to enhance performance and prevent injuries through movement analysis and technique optimisation.  
Physical therapists and Physiotherapists will study the principles of biomechanics in their context.

### **Can a sports biomechanist help with any sport?**

Yes, sports biomechanics principles apply to all sports. A biomechanist can analyze and improve movement patterns in any athletic activity.

### **How often should I see a sports biomechanist?**

The frequency depends on your goals and needs. Regular check-ins can help maintain progress, but even occasional assessments can provide valuable insights.

### **Is sports biomechanics only for elite athletes?**

No, athletes of all levels can benefit from biomechanics. Whether you’re a beginner or a professional, optimising your movements can enhance your performance and reduce injury risk.

How Sports Biomechanics Helps Athletes: Enhancing Performance and Preventing Injuries
=====================================================================================

Author:

Uplift Editorial Team

|

November 18, 2024

Sports biomechanics is a field that blends the principles of mechanics with the complexities of human movement to enhance athletic performance and reduce the risk of injuries. By analyzing the movements of athletes, biomechanics provides insights that help in refining techniques, optimizing equipment, and developing effective training programs. This science is pivotal across various sports, including baseball, basketball, and golf, where subtle changes in motion can lead to significant improvements in performance.

In this article, we'll explore how sports biomechanics assists athletes in reaching their full potential. We'll delve into the ways biomechanics contributes to sports performance, injury prevention, and the specific applications in baseball, basketball, and golf. We'll also discuss how [Uplift](https://www.uplift.ai/products/uplift?utm_source=blog&utm_content=preventinginjures) is making biomechanical analysis more accessible to athletes at all levels.

**Understanding Sports Biomechanics**
-------------------------------------

At its core, biomechanics is the study of the mechanical laws relating to the movement or structure of living organisms. In sports, biomechanics examines the forces exerted by muscles and gravity on the skeletal structure and how these forces affect performance as well as potentially lead to injury. By understanding these principles, athletes and coaches can identify inefficiencies, correct techniques, and enhance overall athletic output.

Biomechanics involves analyzing movements in terms of:

*   **Kinematics**: The study of motion without considering the forces that cause it, focusing on aspects like velocity, acceleration, and displacement.
*   **Kinetics**: The examination of the forces that cause or result from motion, including muscle forces and external forces like gravity.

By applying these analyses, biomechanics helps in optimizing athletic movements to achieve maximum efficiency and effectiveness.

**Enhancing Sports Performance Through Biomechanics**
-----------------------------------------------------

### **Technique Refinement**

One of the primary benefits of biomechanics is the ability to refine an athlete's technique. By closely examining movement patterns, biomechanics can reveal subtle inefficiencies or improper mechanics that might not be apparent to the naked eye. Correcting these issues can lead to improvements in speed, power, and agility.

For example, a basketball player might adjust their shooting form to achieve a more accurate and consistent shot, or a golfer might tweak their swing to increase distance and control.

### **Equipment Optimization**

Biomechanics also plays a crucial role in the design and selection of sports equipment. Understanding how athletes interact with their equipment allows for the development of gear that enhances performance while reducing injury risk.

In baseball, for instance, bat design can be optimized based on biomechanical studies to improve swing speed and ball impact. Similarly, basketball shoes can be engineered to provide better support and traction, tailored to the specific movements of the sport.

### **Personalized Training Programs**

By analyzing an athlete's biomechanical data, coaches can develop training programs that address individual needs. This personalization ensures that athletes focus on areas that will yield the most significant performance gains.

Strength and conditioning programs can be tailored to correct muscle imbalances or enhance specific movements critical to the athlete's sport.

**Injury Prevention and Rehabilitation**
----------------------------------------

Injuries are a significant concern for athletes, often hindering performance and shortening careers. Biomechanics aids in identifying risk factors and developing strategies to prevent injuries.

### **Identifying Risk Factors**

Biomechanical analysis can reveal movement patterns that place excessive stress on joints, muscles, or tendons. For example, in basketball, improper landing techniques after a jump can increase the risk of ankle or knee injuries. By identifying these patterns, corrective measures can be implemented.

### **Developing Preventative Strategies**

Once risk factors are identified, biomechanics helps in designing interventions such as:

*   **Technique Modification**: Adjusting movement patterns to reduce stress on vulnerable areas.
*   **Strengthening Exercises**: Targeting specific muscles to improve support and stability.
*   **Flexibility Training**: Enhancing the range of motion to prevent strains and sprains.

### **Rehabilitation Programs**

For athletes recovering from injuries, biomechanics informs the development of rehabilitation protocols that consider the mechanical stresses on healing tissues, ensuring a safe and effective return to sport.

**Applications in Specific Sports**
-----------------------------------

### **Baseball**

In baseball, biomechanics is essential in analyzing pitching mechanics, batting techniques, and fielding motions. By studying the kinematics and kinetics of these movements, players can enhance performance and reduce the risk of overuse injuries commonly seen in pitchers, such as shoulder and elbow injuries.

Biomechanical analysis can help pitchers refine their delivery to increase velocity while minimizing strain. Batters can adjust their swing mechanics to improve contact and power.

### **Basketball**

Basketball players benefit from biomechanics through improved shooting techniques, jumping ability, and defensive movements. Analyzing the biomechanics of shooting can lead to a more consistent and accurate shot by optimizing release angle and hand positioning.

Understanding the mechanics of jumping and landing can enhance vertical leap and agility while reducing injury risks associated with high-impact movements.

### **Golf**

In golf, the swing is a complex motion that requires precise coordination. Biomechanics helps golfers understand the sequencing of movements from the backswing to the follow-through. By optimizing swing mechanics, golfers can achieve greater distance, accuracy, and consistency.

Biomechanical insights can also prevent common golf injuries, such as lower back pain, by promoting proper posture and swing technique.

**Leveraging Technology with Uplift**
-------------------------------------

Advancements in technology have made biomechanical analysis more accessible to athletes outside of laboratory settings. [Uplift](https://www.uplift.ai/products/uplift?utm_source=blog&utm_content=preventinginjures) utilizes your smartphone to capture and analyze athletic movements, providing detailed biomechanical insights.

### **How Uplift Enhances Biomechanical Analysis**

*   **Ease of Use**: Record movements using your phone's camera without the need for specialized equipment.
*   **Detailed Feedback**: Receive personalized analyses of your technique with suggestions for improvement.
*   **Progress Tracking**: Monitor changes over time to see the impact of adjustments and training.
*   **Accessibility**: Make biomechanical analysis available to athletes at all levels, not just professionals.

By incorporating tools like [Uplift](https://www.uplift.ai/products/uplift?utm_source=blog&utm_content=preventinginjures) into training, athletes can continuously refine their techniques, enhance performance, and reduce injury risks.

**Embracing Biomechanics for Athletic Success**
-----------------------------------------------

Sports biomechanics offers invaluable insights that empower athletes to reach new heights in their performance. By understanding the mechanical principles underlying athletic movements, athletes can refine techniques, prevent injuries, and gain a competitive edge.

Whether you're swinging a bat, shooting a basketball, or perfecting your golf swing, embracing biomechanics can transform your approach to training and competition. With the accessibility of technological tools like Uplift's app, integrating biomechanical analysis into your routine has never been easier.

Investing time in understanding and applying biomechanical principles can lead to significant improvements in sports performance, making it a vital component of modern athletic training.

With extensive experience in biomechanics and sports performance, he has contributed to over 85 peer-reviewed manuscripts and presented at numerous national and international conferences. His expertise ensures that the information provided is grounded in the latest scientific research and best practices in athletic training.

## 

# Sport Biomechanics

![Beth Wilcox, PhD performs biomechanic research with an athlete.](/content/mgb-global/global/en/about/newsroom/articles/biomechanics-in-sports/_jcr_content/root/container_1214295969/image.coreimg.jpeg/1728495738975/cspar-beth-wilcox-biomechanics-2200x1200.jpeg)

Sports biomechanics is the study of athlete movement and the internal and external forces generated by or acting upon the body during sports activities. The application of biomechanics in sport can help athletes reach higher levels of performance while reducing their chance of injury. According to [Beth Wilcox, PhD](/en/patient-care/services-and-specialties/sports-medicine/center-for-sports-performance-and-research/team/beth-wilcox), sports medicine researcher at Mass General Brigham’s [Center for Sports Performance and Research](/en/patient-care/services-and-specialties/sports-medicine/center-for-sports-performance-and-research), "Professional sports teams have recognized the value of biomechanical applications in sport, and many now have full-time biomechanists on staff. Studying how athletes move, whether they are seasoned professionals or amateurs just starting out, can provide valuable lessons that help unlock potential and improve performance exponentially.”

## What is biomechanics?

Biomechanics is a scientific field that applies the laws of mechanics and physics to the movement of living things. At its broadest level, it includes everything from learning how muscle cells contract, to analyzing the gait of a running horse, to understanding the complex, full-body movements of a dancer.

Biomechanics in sports is a narrower field that provides valuable information to athletes, coaches, and sports medicine professionals to optimize performance and reduce the risk of injury. Biomechanical assessments can identify inefficient movement patterns, quantify rotational forces at individual joints, identify muscle imbalances, monitor fatigue and measure improvement of an athlete's movement quality during rehabilitation.

### Kinetics vs. kinematics

Kinetics and kinematics are two important sub-categories of biomechanics. Kinetics examines the forces that cause motion, such as muscular force and gravity, while kinematics quantifies the properties of movement, such as velocity and acceleration.

### The importance of biomechanics in sports

There are three main ways that biomechanics is useful in sports:

Beth Wilcox, PhD  
Research Scientist  
Mass General Brigham

## Biomechanical analysis in sport

Biomechanical analysis in sport takes many forms. A coach or medical professional visually assessing a runner's gait on a treadmill is a simple form of sports biomechanics. More advanced forms of biomechanical analysis integrate advanced technology to gain a deeper understanding of athletic movement than is achievable using the unaided human eye.

### Biomechanical technology in sports

Biomechanical technology includes a wide variety of devices, including:

![A monitor gathers biomechanic data on an athlete.](/content/mgb-global/global/en/about/newsroom/articles/biomechanics-in-sports/_jcr_content/root/container_1214295969/image_1784028616.coreimg.jpeg/1728495739030/cspar-monitor-track-1070x700.jpeg)

## FAQs about the biomechanics of sport and exercise

### How is biomechanics used in sports?

Biomechanics is used to analyze an athlete's movements and the forces produced by and acting on that athlete’s body. This analysis can help the athlete improve their technique, optimize performance, and/or minimize the risk of injury. Biomechanics can also be used to understand the relationship between the athlete, their environment, and the equipment they use. Biomechanical testing is used in the design and development of sporting equipment such as footwear, apparel, protective equipment, wearables, prosthetics, and adaptive equipment for athletes with disabilities.

### How can biomechanics be useful to athletic coaches and trainers?

Coaches can use the principles of biomechanics in sport to analyze player performance, identify inefficient technique, help players prevent or recover from injuries, and develop conditioning protocols that target athletes' areas of weakness.

### What are some examples of biomechanics in everyday life?

Biomechanics is relevant to more than just sports. Most things we interact with can be understood biomechanically. For instance, automotive designers must consider the way human bodies move to build cars that are comfortable and easy to use. Biomechanics also explains many common movement problems: "text neck" is a perfect example of a biomechanical problem induced by the way we use an everyday object like a cell phone. "Text neck" refers to the strain and discomfort in the neck and upper back caused by looking down at mobile devices for extended periods. This posture can lead to muscle fatigue and pain.

### What are biomechanics exercises?

Your movements—even those you think about and practice in athletic training—are the result of habits. These habits can be good (like squatting with your knees in line with your toes) or bad (lifting with your back). Biomechanics exercises are usually repeated exercises that encourage optimal movement patterns and help you develop better habits.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biomechanics analyzes athletes' movements, identifying inefficiencies in technique to enhance performance, speed, and power through optimized mechanics critical for skill development and execution.
By assessing movement patterns, biomechanics helps identify risk factors for injuries, allowing for corrective strategies that mitigate excessive stress on joints and improve overall safety.
Biomechanics informs the design of sports equipment tailored to athletes’ movements, enhancing performance through better gear like footwear and gear that reduces injury risk and improves comfort.
